Class TaskScheduleService

java.lang.Object
de.hallerweb.enterprise.prioritize.service.scheduling.TaskScheduleService
@Service @Transactional public class TaskScheduleService extends Object
Orchestrates recurring TaskSchedules: computes their cron cadence and, when due, generates tasks from their template via TaskService.createScheduledTask(Project, String, String, int).

Firing is done by runDueSchedules(LocalDateTime), a plain method that a poller (a separate slice) calls on a fixed cadence — this class itself does no background scheduling. For each due schedule it creates the task through the trusted, user-less path, records lastFiredAt and advances nextFireAt to the next cron occurrence. A failure on one schedule is logged and isolated so the rest of the batch still fires.

Zones: a schedule's cron is evaluated in its own

invalid reference 
zone
(or the server zone when blank), but nextFireAt is stored normalized to the server zone so the poller can compare every schedule against a single LocalDateTime.now() regardless of per-schedule zones. See nextFireAfter(String, ZoneId, ZoneId, LocalDateTime).

Administration: create/read/update/delete over a project's schedules. A schedule is durable project configuration that keeps producing tasks unattended, so mutations are manager only (like project goals and the project's team/resource/document management), while reading is open to every member. Authorization is delegated to ProjectService — the same membership model as the rest of the project API, with no admin bypass.

Author:
peter haller

  • Constructor Details

    • TaskScheduleService

      public TaskScheduleService()

  • Method Details

    • runDueSchedules

      public int runDueSchedules(LocalDateTime now)
      Fires every enabled schedule that is due at or before now (server-zone wall clock): generates a task from its template, stamps lastFiredAt and advances nextFireAt. Failures are isolated per schedule.
      Parameters:
      now - the current server-zone timestamp
      Returns:
      the number of schedules that fired successfully

    • nextFireFor

      public LocalDateTime nextFireFor(TaskSchedule schedule, LocalDateTime fromServerLocal)
      The next fire time for a schedule after fromServerLocal, normalized to the server zone. The schedule's own zone (or the server zone when blank) drives the cron's wall-clock meaning.
      Returns:
      the next occurrence, or null if the cron has none

    • createSchedule

      public TaskScheduleDTO createSchedule(Long projectId, TaskScheduleRequest req, PUser user)
      Creates a schedule on a project. Manager only. An enabled schedule gets its nextFireAt computed right away, so it starts firing without waiting for an update.
      Throws:
      NoSuchElementException - if the project does not exist
      org.springframework.security.access.AccessDeniedException - if the user is not the project's manager
      IllegalArgumentException - if the request is incomplete or the cron/zone is invalid

    • getSchedules

      @Transactional(readOnly=true) public List<TaskScheduleDTO> getSchedules(Long projectId, PUser user)
      Lists a project's schedules (enabled or not). Any member or the manager may read.

    • getSchedule

      @Transactional(readOnly=true) public TaskScheduleDTO getSchedule(Long id, PUser user)
      Reads a single schedule. Any member or the manager of its project may read.

    • updateSchedule

      public TaskScheduleDTO updateSchedule(Long id, TaskScheduleRequest patch, PUser user)
      Applies a partial update (only non-null request fields). Manager only. nextFireAt is recomputed when the cadence changed or the schedule was (re-)enabled — an unrelated edit (a rename, a new task description) deliberately leaves a pending fire time untouched so it cannot be pushed past its due moment.

    • deleteSchedule

      public void deleteSchedule(Long id, PUser user)
      Deletes a schedule. Manager only. Already generated tasks are not touched.
